steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: The student wants to "compare the lengths of the two rail trails." An effective comparison must state both lengths explicitly and express a clear relationship between them. The notes give: Mickelson Trail = 109 miles; Cannon Valley Trail = 19.7 miles.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — Similar to the Cannon Valley Trail, the 109-mile-long George S. Mickelson Trail was created from a disused railroad corridor.: Mentions only the Mickelson Trail's length and focuses on shared origin, not length comparison. Omission rule violated. ✗
  • Choice B — Although they are made of different materials, the George S. Mickelson Trail and the Cannon Valley Trail are nearly identical in length.: Claims the trails are "nearly identical in length," which is false — 109 miles vs. 19.7 miles is a large difference. Factual accuracy rule violated. ✗
  • Choice C — Rail trails of different lengths can be found in South Dakota and Minnesota.: Mentions neither specific length, making no meaningful comparison. Omission rule violated. ✗
  • Choice D — At 109 miles long, the George S. Mickelson Trail is much longer than the 19.7-mile Cannon Valley Trail.: States both specific lengths and directly compares them with a clear directional claim ("much longer"). ✓
That confirms D is correct.
